transcript: Hip replacement for patients with osteoporosis.  This is a concern that many people have, especially women over 60, particularly women who begin to develop osteopenia first, which is an earlier stage, and then develop osteoporosis.  So, even in people with osteopenia, it's important that we have specific strategies in place in case that patient needs a hip replacement.  And this is very common, more common than you think, especially because most people who will need hip replacements are over 60 years old, the majority.  Of course, as a hip specialist, I end up treating younger people, because many colleagues who don't want to treat younger patients refer them to me, and I 'm a reference in this type of surgery, especially with the use of robotics, where we can make a prosthesis perform at its maximum even in a young person.  But today's topic is osteoporosis, osteopenia, and hip replacement.  Can a person with osteopenia or osteoporosis safely undergo hip replacement surgery?  So the answer is yes.  However, there are strategies and plans in place to make this surgery safer.  And today I'm going to take an example case, which is a case of extreme osteoporosis in an 85-year-old patient, okay?  85 years old.  So, I'm going to show you the case of this real patient I operated on, and I'm going to show you my planning decisions and the types of materials and strategies that were used to ensure the best chance of success for a patient who already has more fragile health and deserves a much higher level of attention.  So, I'm going to look at the illustrations here because I've already prepared them to show you what we're seeing here.  We are therefore looking at an X-ray of an 85-year-old patient with extreme arthrosis and osteoporosis that has caused a hip protrusion.  The hip protruded inward on both sides, and this hip protrusion was so extreme that on the left side, which is this one here, it even ruptured the bony wall of the acetabulum.  So, the degree of osteoporosis was so severe that it caused an insufficiency fracture of the medial wall of the acetabulum.  So, how should a surgeon prepare to help a patient with this degree of osteoporosis, a patient who has been using a walker and wheelchair for several years?  She was constantly using a walker and a wheelchair so that she could walk again and increase her level of independence.  So here are planning strategies, and I'm going to show you my strategy and why I made each choice in planning for this patient, so we can greatly increase the chance of success.  So the first point we have to resolve here, and it's the most important point, is the bottom of the cetabulum, which is torn on one side.  The other side is also torn, which I could see on the CT scan.  It doesn't look like it here, but the CT scan shows that it was already there.  ...and the significant shortening that occurred on both sides, as well as the medialization. So, the femur bone of this patient, it moved up and went towards the medial part, it went into the skin, right? So, there was a rise and mediation on both sides.  So, the first point we need to address is this upper part here, which is the acetabulum.  So, I need an implant that gives me great security in its fixation, since the bone isn't of very good quality due to osteoporosis, I have to use a special implant that provides more stability.  I also need to use an implant that allows me to insert screws if necessary.  I also need an implant that gives me maximum stability, because an 85- year-old woman doesn't have very strong muscles , especially considering she's been using a walker and wheelchair for about two years. Her muscles are also weak.  So I need to secure the implant and ensure stability.  Why is fixation an important point?  Because for an 85-year-old lady, it's also very difficult for her not to give her full support.  It's difficult for a person to support their weight using their arms in a walker.  So I have to use an implant that gives me a very strong initial absolute fixation so that the patient feels safe putting all their weight on the operated leg, both in relation to the acetabulum and the femoral part.  So let's look at some strategies that have been used so you can learn about these strategies and feel calmer, knowing that medicine is evolving and we have more and more strategies to help in cases like this.  So, the first point here is to talk to you about trabecular metal.  This top part here is an acetabular component, the one that goes into the socket up there in the acetabulum, where it has a special metal, which is a metal that helps the bone grow more in the implant.  This was initially developed by one company many years ago, and then several other companies copied this type of metal.  And today it is called trabecular metal.  And each company has a specific name for its implant model.  Here is a microscopy image showing that this metal is extremely porous.  And this porosity is important for inducing bone growth.  Here's an electron microscopy image showing that the trabecular meshwork of this bone material is very similar to the trabecular meshwork of our bone.  So here 's a drawing showing the trabecular meshwork of metal integrated into the trabecular meshwork of a bone with osteoporosis. But this is a drawing.  Now here is also an illustration, showing the trabecular meshwork of the metal integrating with the trabecular meshwork of the bone.  But here, this is no longer a drawing, this is an electron microscopy image showing the implant design and the bone merging with the implant.  This type of implant is a solution when we have large bone defects, especially above 20%, when we have hip dysplasia, or when we have some childhood sequelae, such as leg injuries, where sometimes the acetabulum is also somewhat dysplastic.  It can also be used when dealing with the after-effects of hip trauma .  So basically, when we have a lack of bone coverage or bone fragility, that's when this type of implant should be considered .  And here it's important to explain to you that this implant becomes more expensive because it's made of a different material, with a different porosity.  This often has to be justified to health insurance plans so that the auditor, who does n't know you, has never seen your X-ray, doesn't know you, and sometimes is trying to choose the implant for the patient, which is extremely wrong, because the doctor knows the patient, knows the bone density, does the planning, and the surgeon is the person who is recognized by the Federal Council of Medicine and recognized as having the best ability to choose the best implant for the patient.  Moving past this initial phase, we're showing this integration.  So this is a well-established thing, it's nothing new.  This implant has the greatest fixation capacity in the situations that have been discussed.  Now, remember that I said that an 85-year-old patient, especially one who has been using a walker and crutches for 2 years, or rather, a wheelchair, also has weakened muscles.  So, in general, patients who may have neuromuscular diseases, muscle weakness, patients generally speaking over 70 years of age, especially women. Of course, not everyone has the same condition, right?  There are people in their 70s who are in excellent shape, but people who have some degree of neuromuscular fragility, that is, primarily muscular or neurological fragility that causes muscle weakness, should also be considered for dual-mobility implants.  This dual-mobility implant means that the implant will rotate between the head and another polyethylene head, and then between that polyethylene head and the acetabular implant. This type of implant, called dual mobility, reduces the risk of dislocation. He has some problems for a very young patient.  So, in a very young patient, it can lead to greater wear and tear, which is undesirable.  But for a 70-year-old patient, the increased wear and tear will not impact or change the implant's durability; the implant will outlive the patient, even if the patient lives past 100 years of age.  So this is a strategy to increase security.  Sometimes the patient has a caregiver, sometimes the patient is in a nursing home, they don't have the strength, and there is a higher risk of falls.  So, a number of situations where we can increase patient safety, this too, unfortunately, can increase the cost of the implant.  And, once again, it has to be explained and justified to the health insurance company.  Obviously, if it were up to me, all implants would cost the same , but here I have to present the reality to you: many times we have to make choices weighing risks and benefits, and divide the risk- benefit with the patients.  When the surgeon shares these risks and benefits with the patient, it becomes an easier choice, okay?  And this choice becomes easier because it's paying a little more for peace of mind and to reduce the risk of an event such as hip prosthesis dislocation.  So this is also a strategy that deserves to be considered.  Now we're going to move on to another point.  We talked about the acetabular component of the upper part, but there's also the lower part, which is the femur. So, for the femoris, we have two main types of systems that can be used.  We can divide prostheses into two main groups.  These are cemented prostheses and uncemented prostheses .  I already have a video on YouTube where I explain osteoporosis and hip replacement.  So, for anyone who wants to see some details about some of the things I'm going to talk about now, and who wants to see the studies that were cited—a very large study—you can watch this other video, okay?  If you type Davi Guzmão, osteoporosis, hip prosthesis, you'll find the video correctly.  I'll try to put it in the description here as well for a patient who we already know [music] is 85 years old and has that degree of osteoporosis in the femoral area, and also has a greater degree of fragility in the femoral area.  Therefore, the choice of a cemented implant should be made here .  What does a cemented implant mean?  It is an implant where there will be an interface between the implant and the patient's bone. In an uncemented prosthesis, the prosthesis must fit snugly against the bone.  And in a patient who has osteoporosis, that bone is more fragile.  The fact that the surgeon needs to insert the prosthesis very snugly increases the risks associated with fracture, especially in this population that has osteoporosis.  This isn't just my opinion; it's based on a very large study that was conducted, which I mention in another video.  So, in order to reduce the risk of fracture, and also considering the bone shape for fixation, the uncemented prosthesis fixes better in certain types of bone shapes.  I won't go into detail here, but for this population that we already know has more severe osteoporosis, a cemented prosthesis will be safer from the point of view of fixation and immediate support.  That's another point, because an 85-year-old patient, like the example, sometimes doesn't have the arm strength to do the partial weight-bearing that the surgeon who performed an uncemented prosthesis often asks the patient to do. If I use a cemented prosthesis, I can bear 100% of my weight without restriction .  Since this cement, which isn't actually cement as we might imagine, is a resin— methyl methacrylate, polymethyl methacrylate— it's a resin that will dry instantly and set within about 9 minutes.  So, 9 minutes after the fixation, the patient can put all their weight on the operated leg, which gives the patient greater freedom to walk, greater freedom to put weight on it, and also helps to reduce risks related to patient immobility, which is very important.  And this implant, it has a high degree of safety, even in cases of osteoporosis so severe that it caused a fracture of the femur, right? So, femoral neck fractures are a situation where the patient had such severe osteoporosis that they fractured their femoral neck.  Therefore, it can be used in cases of fracture, in primary cases, and also in cases of revision hip replacement.  Of course, in this case, revision is very specific; each type of bone shape has to be analyzed.  So this is a very particular situation when choosing the best implant.  And nowadays implants come in various sizes.  Back in the day, a long time ago, folks, prostheses came in small, medium, and large sizes.   The 80s were small, medium, large. Imagine how difficult it is to find the specific implant for each patient.  And nowadays we have various implant sizes to find the ideal implant for each bone shape.  Because just as we have different characteristics in terms of hand size, foot size, width, etc.  Our bones also have different characteristics, in addition to the consequences of childhood, as well as those related to osteoarthritis and osteoporosis.  So, we have a range of implant sizes to choose from.  And here, speaking about fixation, we're showing that cemented prostheses in patients over 75 years old, when compared to the three best uncemented implants, showed that when choosing the uncemented implant in this study, there is a nine times greater chance of needing to replace the implant, which is a revision.  This was chosen from among the three best cemented implants.  So, this study here, and it's not the only study that exists, right?  Here are a number of bibliographic references on the subject; it's not the only study, but it shows that in a specific population with a degree of osteoporosis or osteopenia, there is a great benefit in using this type of strategy.  Let's go back to the case that started this discussion.  So, let's remember here an 85-year-old lady, both sides affected, and here's the strategy that was used.  So, she has a trabicular metal implant on both sides.  One side required a screw, the other did not.  Both are cemented to ensure immediate support.  And the patient can receive immediate, but limited, support.  And today she walks so much that the problem, what hurts the most, is her knee and not her hips anymore.  So look at how dramatic the situation was before, with a setabular protrusion, including a defect and fracture at the base.  A graft was placed at the bottom of both acetabula.  This is a recent post-operative image, I believe it's about a month post-surgery.  A graft was placed at the bottom of both acetabula to make the correction.  Note that the separation of the femur bone from the pelvis is now more within the normal range, whereas before they were overlapping.  And note that we also had distalization of the femur. So, the length of her legs increased because she had lost some, and the femur shifted laterally, giving more muscle tension because it was very shortened.  Of course, this tightening of the muscles already helps a lot from a stability standpoint, but better safe than sorry.  We have no margin for error in an 85-year-old patient.  An 85- year-old patient is not immune to experimentation. We need to provide the highest possible guarantee of success.  And through careful planning, the chance of success becomes very, very high.  So, everyone, I'm leaving this open in the comments for you to ask any questions you may have about this case study and explanations regarding trabecular metal, dual mobility, and cemented implants.  Until the next video.

Among the treatment options for hip osteoarthritis, we have several treatment modalities.  Within infiltrative treatments, which involve placing a product inside the cartilage, or rather , inside the joint, placing a product here inside the joint, we basically have two major divisions of products.  We have hyaluronic acid and bioactive hydrogel.  Today I'm going to talk a little about the differences between the two.  When do I usually recommend one product over another? My name is David Guzmão, I am an orthopedic surgeon specializing in the hip joint.  So let's understand hyaluronic acid and bioactive hydrogel within the context of cartilage wear in the hip and knee.  Hyaluronic acid is a molecule that our body produces to lubricate our synovial joints.  However, when we experience some degree of wear and tear, a decline in the concentration of this substance begins to occur.  The goal of the therapy is to place a more concentrated form of hyaluronic acid within the joint so that a biological stimulus occurs, causing the body to produce more hyaluronic acid.  This occurs to lubricate the cartilage, improve cartilage hydration, and modulate the inflammatory reaction resulting from osteoarthritis within that joint.  These are the goals of doing this therapy with ceruronic acid, and it shows results.  We've been doing knee joint reconstruction for many years, and for about 10 or 12 years now, we've started doing it on other joints as well, like the hip, ankle, shoulder, elbow, and even small joints like the wrist joints.  My area of ​​expertise is the hip and knee area, so I administer injections in the hip and knee. And where does bioactive hydrogel fit into all this ?  Bioactive hydrogel is a type of hyaluronic acid modified to have greater durability within the joint, providing a mechanical cushioning effect due to its larger molecular shape compared to hyaluronic acid, and also a greater biological effect than hyaluronic acid.  This has been proven through studies showing a comparison between bioactive hyaluronic acid hydrogel and the knee.  In fact, the package insert indicates its use in cases of higher degrees of osteoarthritis when applied to the knee, and it also indicates its use in treating degenerative lesions of the knee.  In the hip, we don't have a meniscus, but we do have a structure, this little white band here, which is analogous to the meniscus in the knee, called the labrum.  Therefore, it is very likely that for those who have some type of degenerative labral lesion, the bioactive hydrogel will have better functionality.  It's impossible to say for sure because a comparative study of hyaluronic acid versus bioactive hydrogel, specifically for labral lesions, hasn't been done.  But if we make an analogy with the knee, perhaps that's true.  Now, my inclination is that when the patient has higher degrees of wear and tear in the hip joint, it is more interesting to consider bioactive hydrogel.  Or when the patient has already had a hyaluronic acid injection and it didn't have as satisfactory an effect, with a shorter duration than expected. In most cases of osteoarthritis, say grade 2 or grade 3, I would expect a duration of between 1 year and 6 months.  If the patient is experiencing shorter-lasting results than expected with hyaluronic acid, I usually recommend considering bioactive hydrogel.  There are two ways to apply bioactive hydrogel to the joint.  Of course, I administer both bioactive hydrogel and hyaluronic acid under ultrasound guidance.  We have an ultrasound device that will show us where we need to administer the medication, because this gives us a much greater guarantee of accuracy .  So, what's the difference in application?  The difference is that, in most cases, it may be more beneficial to split the application of bioactive hydrogels, applying half the dose and then, after a month, applying the other half.  Since it's a product with a higher concentration and viscosity, administering two separate applications at slightly different times—perhaps three weeks or a month apart— can have a superior effect. This patient group, in particular, experiences greater wear and tear.  Now, if the patient, for example, is of a good height, which means that the hip joint will be larger, and they don't experience very intense wear and tear, they understand that this single-dose application might be a little more uncomfortable .  The bioactive hydrogel can be made in a single dose.  So much so that in my hip and knee, I administered both doses at the same time.  Now, in those other situations I mentioned, it might be more interesting to do it in two stages.  Take half the dose now and take the other half dose in about a month .  Therefore, this subgroup of patients may experience a longer- lasting effect and greater symptom relief.  So, it's good to consider that.  But these recommendations will be made on a case-by-case basis.  I analyze the patient's exam, the physical exam, the MRI scan, the X-ray, and this will help us decide what the best therapy logistics are.  I also take into consideration the distance where the patient lives.  Sometimes it's difficult for the patient to come twice, and with that in mind, a single application might also be beneficial .  When a patient has never undergone any of the therapies and has a mild grade two to three alteration, it may be more appropriate to start with luronic acid.  As one patient told me today: "Dor Guzmão, I want to start with anionic acid because I don't want to waste my resources." Bicicleta para quem tem artrose ou desgaste no quadril joelho. Qual o melhor tipo de bicicleta é possível fazer? Qual a melhor estratégia para se exercitar sem machucar mais a articulação? Olá, meu nome é David Guzmão, sou médico ortopedista focado na articulação do quadril e joelho. Trabalho muito com a cartilagem, que é, afinal de contas, um tecido extremamente especializado que serve para diminuir o atrito das nossas articulações. E a bicicleta é um tipo de exercício aeróbico que pode auxiliar muito na lubrificação das articulações, no fortalecimento e tem menos impacto. Então, vamos entender os tipos de bicicleta de acordo com o tipo de artrose que a pessoa apresenta. Mas em primeiro lugar, vamos entender o que que é artrose. Artrose é o desgaste da cartilagem, que é um tecido que nós temos na ponta dos nossos ossos, que serve para que ocorra um deslizamento suave nas articulações. E algumas vezes pode acontecer o desgaste dessa cartilagem devido a vários fatores. Fatores de alteração de formato de uma articulação, fatores relacionados a trauma, doenças da infância, genética, da idade. Então, tem muitas coisas que podem levar a ter um desgaste. O tipo de bicicleta vai ter relação com a localização do desgaste. Então, para quem se interessa para esse assunto, já se inscreve no canal, porque eu trago aqui toda semana as melhores informações com baseado em ciência e relação ao desgaste de artros de quadril e de joelho. Então vamos entrar nesse ponto aqui. A gente tem que subdividir o tipo de desgaste em três: desgaste do quadril, desgaste do joelho entre a tíbia e o fêmor, que normalmente fala desgaste do joelho, mas tem um tipo de desgaste no joelho específico que se chama desgaste da articulação fêmoropatelar, que é aquele ossinho que a gente tem na frente do joelho, que é a patela parece uma capinha, esse é um tipo de desgaste um pouquinho diferente e se comporta um pouco diferente em relação à bicicleta. A bicicleta nós temos também três tipos, né? Temos a bicicleta vertical, aquela que imita uma bicicleta normal de rua, a bicicleta normal de rua e a bicicleta horizontal, aquela que a pessoa senta num numa região que parece uma cadeira como essa que eu tô sentado aqui e que ela parece ter um conforto maior, mas não é tão boa assim para todas as articulações. Então vamos lá. Artrose de quadril. Quem tem artrose de quadril usualmente pode fazer bicicleta e usualmente a bicicleta do tipo vertical, que é aquela que é parecida com a bicicleta de rua, será melhor. Por quê? Na bicicleta vertical ocorre uma elevação menor do joelho em relação ao tronco do que na bicicleta horizontal. Inclusive, uma das táticas para diminuir a amplitude de flexão do quadril é se levantar mais o banco do que o usual, levantar o máximo o banco do que a pessoa consegue ainda alcançar no pedal. Dessa forma, o joelho levanta e é mais tranquilo fazer atividade física na bicicleta. É natural observar, e eu quero que você me conte no comentário se algum de vocês observa isso, que quando faz a bicicleta e já tem algum grau de artrose, às vezes o joelho abre ao pedalar. Na medida que a pessoa pedala, em vez de conseguir pedalar com o joelho retinho pra frente, o joelho abre um pouquinho para fora para pedalar. Isso é sinal de que o grau da artose tá sendo suficientemente intenso para modificar a biomecânica daquela articulação, no caso da articulação do quadril. Então, às vezes o quadril abre para fora. Agora, tão benéfico quanto a bicicleta ergométrica de academia vertical, também pode ser feito a bicicleta na rua. Aí entra o cuidado de cada pessoa em relação ao seu equilíbrio, seu ambiente, porque a bicicleta tá muito associado a riscos de acidente, risco de queda e risco de colisão com outros veículos. Então você tem que saber o que que vai ser melhor para você. Por via das dúvidas, a bicicleta vertical dentro de uma academia, fisioterapia, ela vai ser mais segura. Entra aqui também, tô falando da artros do quadril no momento, a questão do equilíbrio de cada pessoa. Porque se uma pessoa não tem um equilíbrio suficiente para ter conforto em cima do banco da bicicleta vertical, uma pessoa de mais idade, pode ser mais desafiador e talvez tenha que se testar usar a bicicleta horizontal. No caso da bicicleta horizontal, se a pessoa não consegue usar a vertical, a tática é colocar o banco mais para trás possível enquanto a pessoa consegue alcançar os pedais. Esse é o ideal. E tem também aquelas bicicletinhas pequeninhas, né, que melhor do que nada, mas ela faz um efeito muito pequeno em termos de movimentação da articulação, em termos de fortalecimento da musculatura que envolve a articulação do quadril do joelho. Ela trabalha pouco a musculatura, mas é melhor que nada. Melhor do que nada, sem sombra de dúvidas. Então, artrose do quadril é melhor a bicicleta vertical. Se a pessoa não tem equilíbrio, pode fazer horizontal. E a vantagem é que a bicicleta tem menos impacto sobre a articulação tanto do quadril do joelho, porque vai ter um movimento mais controlado, sem aquele impacto da caminhada, especialmente para quem tá com dificuldade de fazer caminhada bicicleta, pode ser um ótimo aliado. Agora vamos falar da articulação do joelho. Na articulação do joelho, da artrose do tipo fêmor tibial, que é entre o fêmor e a tíbia, também vale a mesma recomendação pro quadril. A bicicleta é um ótimo exercício. A bicicleta vertical costuma ser melhor. Não tem tanto problema quanto quadril bicicleta horizontal. Então não se estresse com isso. Se você consegue fazer a vertical horizontal, não tem problema. A bicicleta rua na rua também não. Porém ocorre aqui uma observação, que é quando a artrose do joelho está ocorrendo na articulação fêmoropatelar, que entre o fêmor e a patela. Nessa situação, quanto maior o grau o grau de flexão no joelho, tende a incomodar mais. Não quer dizer que seja proibitivo, longe disso. Agora, conforme o grau de artrose ou de condromalácia, muitas pessoas já ouviram falar nesse termo, pode incomodar a bicicleta. Então, também vale nessa situação, quanto mais alto o banco na bicicleta vertical ou quanto mais para trás na bicicleta horizontal, mais confortável vai ser pedalar. Para quem tem artros fêmoropatelar, dos três tipos de artros que foram citadas no quadril, na fêmor tibial, no joelho, na fêmoropatelar, artros fêmoropatelar é a que pode ser mais desafiadora para usar a bicicleta, porque a artrose fomemoropatelar é aquela artose que doe mais ao subir e descer a escada. No caminhar no plano não incomoda muito. Então escreve aqui nos comentários, ó, quem tá nessa situação. Dói para subir e descer a escada, mas caminhar no plano não dói nada, que a artrose fomoropatelar ela costuma se comportar dessa maneira. Então, para quem tem artrose fomoropatelar, talvez seja mais interessante a pessoa fazer caminhada na esteira ou na rua do que a bicicleta. Então, não é que a bicicleta seja proibitivo, a gente tem que adaptar a realidade de cada pessoa, mas pode ter uma tendência de ser mais confortável pra pessoa caminhar no plano ou numa esteira ou na rua para quem tem artros fêmor patatelar. Agora, pessoal, às vezes as coisas vêm combinadas, né? Então, às vezes a pessoa tem artros fêmoropatelar, artros furorotibial no joelho e tem um pouquinho de artrose no quadril também. As coisas pode estar combinada. Nessa situação, a gente vai ter que testar o que vai ser melhor para cada pessoa, individualizar a recomendação ou até mesmo fazer uma combinação. Um dia faz uma caminhada, um dia faz uma bicicleta e faz uma variabilidade. O importante é manter um movimento. Agora, às vezes a artrose não tá só no quadril ou só no joelho, às vezes a artrose também está na coluna lombar. Para quem tem artrose na coluna lombar, a bicicleta vertical costuma incomodar um pouco. Claro, não tô falando apenas a pessoa ter artrose, porque muitos de nós têm artrose na coluna lombar. Eu tô me referindo ao fato da pessoa ter artrose na coluna lombar e ter dor resultante dessa artrose ou uma protusão de scal ou algum componente neural de dores da lombar. Isso também pode ser comprometido quando a pessoa faz a bicicleta, especialmente a bicicleta vertical. Então, notem que o nosso corpo ele tá todo interconectado e a gente tem que fazer a recomendação melhor de acordo com a estratégia e de acordo com a alteração que cada pessoa apresenta. Mas o principal recado desse vídeo é o seguinte: movimento ajuda pra articulação. É melhor você se movimentar da maneira que cause menos desconforto. E aí tem escolha de caminhada, bicicleta vertical, bicicleta na rua ou bicicleta horizontal. Todo mundo consegue fazer algum grau de movimento. Se a pessoa não consegue, ela tá num extremo de artros que precisa de um tratamento o quanto antes. Só que vai ter alguma situação que a pessoa tá numa artrose, digamos assim, num grau trê três mais que tá com dificuldade de fazer essas atividades, mas ainda tem um movimento bom na articulação em termos de amplitude de movimento. E às vezes a pessoa até tá se preparando para uma cirurgia dali a 6 meses, dali a 1 ano, seja no quadro joelho. Só que ela tá tendo dificuldade de fazer essas atividades, de fortalecer na academia, fortalecer na fisioterapia, tá tendo dificuldade porque dói. Essa é uma situação que pode se lançar mão de uma terapia que é terapia infiltrativa com ácido hialurônico ou com o hidrogel bioativo, colocando esse suplemento articular dentro da articulação, dentro do quadril, dentro do joelho. Isso pode ajudar a pessoa a se preparar melhor para uma cirurgia. pode ajudar uma pessoa que tem um grau dois, por exemplo, de um de artros, no joelho, poder ficar completamente assintomática, porque tanto ácido hialurônico quanto hidrogel bioativo, eles conseguem lentificar o avanço da artrose nessas articulações. Vídeo no canal falando a diferença entre o ácido hialurônico e entre o hidrogel bioativo. Basicamente, o hidrogel bioativo é uma evolução do ácido hialurônico, tá? tem uma potência maior, uma durabilidade maior. E isso pode ser, pode ser, não, isso é uma ferramenta no tratamento da artros e uma das ferramentas não cirúrgicas mais potentes que deve ser utilizada junto com fortalecimento, com emagrecimento, estratégias alimentares, dietas anti-inflamatórias, tudo isso pode fazer parte de uma estratégia não cirúrgica ou preparatória para uma cirurgia ter o maior sucesso possível no paciente. A very common mistake for people with osteoarthritis is waiting until the pain gets worse before doing something about it .  So let me tell you a truth that most doctors don't have time to explain in their office. Every month you wait, your cartilage loses ground that it may never recover.  But here's some good news, and that's why I recorded this video.  Science is closer than ever to truly regenerating cartilage .  The problem is that you need to have spare cartilage for when this technology arrives.  In this video, I'm going to show you exactly what to do today with existing treatments and changes that depend only on you to keep your joint alive while science races to find the definitive solution.  And at the end of the video, I'm going to show you the Stanford research that is changing everything we know about cartilage regeneration.  It's all the rage in Silicon Valley.  My name is David Gosmão, I am an orthopedic doctor specializing in hip and knee cartilage joints. I practice in Porto Alegre, and this channel exists to provide you with quality information based on science and without beating around the bush.  Before discussing treatment, I need you to understand one simple thing about arthritis.  Think of cartilage as a shock absorber for the joint.  It's that smooth, tough layer that covers the bone and allows it to slide over the other.  Here we have an example of good cartilage.  And this is where the cartilage begins to wear down, which is something we don't want to happen. So, osteoarthritis is the progressive wear and tear of this shock absorber, and it starts with small cracks on the surface, then deepens, until in advanced cases the bone becomes exposed and begins to rub against each other.  And here's the key point.  Cartilage does not have blood vessels, and the body has a very limited ability to regenerate it. In other words, if you lose, you don't recover easily.  That's why the time to act is now, not when you're in unbearable pain and have significant limitations.  And that's where the biggest mistake I see in my office every day comes in.  It's the mistake of waiting for the pain to get worse. I hear this every week in my office. Doctor, but I'm still fine.  The pain comes on occasionally, does it need treatment already? And I understand, it seems logical, if it doesn't hurt much, why mess with it?  But this is precisely where osteoarthritis betrays its true nature, because the pain from osteoarthritis is not proportional to the damage that is occurring.  When you start to feel constant pain, the cartilage may have lost a very significant amount of tissue.  It's like a tooth with a face; sometimes when it really hurts, the hole in the tooth is already too big.  And here's a concept I want you to remember: the therapeutic window of opportunity.  There is a stage of osteoarthritis: grade one, grade two, and the beginning of grade three.  In this stage, grade 1, 2, and the beginning of grade 3, conservative treatments work very well.  If you miss that window of opportunity, your options dwindle and a prosthetic arm becomes the most likely path.  Let me show you in practice what happens when someone treats you at the right time.  And I'm going to tell you about a clinical case that repeats itself every week.  I'll give an example using a fictional name, but this story happened exactly a week ago.  And I'll give you an example of a woman, something that happened to her a week ago, but it's even more frequent than with men.  I'll tell you about a typical patient, I'll call her Maria, 49 years old, a dentist, she loved walking in the park and started feeling a slight discomfort in her hip, a little discomfort in her knee, when she lifted a chair, a ladder, sometimes when getting in and out of the car, and she waited.  She thought, "Oh, it's just a thing of old age, a little scrape on my knee."  But after a year, the discomfort she felt occasionally turned into pain when walking, and after two years, she stopped walking altogether.  And when she arrived at my office, the X-ray showed grade three osteoarthritis with significant cartilage loss. Now imagine a different scenario.  If this woman, Maria, 49 years old, had sought help when the discomfort began. Grade two osteoarthritis, cartilage with good thickness.  At that time, a treatment protocol with viscosupplementation, hyaluronic acid, and bioactive hydrogel could have made a very big difference to the joint.  Now let's look at this scenario.  She underwent therapy with hyaluronic acid, bioactive hydrogel, muscle strengthening, and weight management. As a result, two years later, the cartilage remained stable on the MRI and she was able to walk again without a prosthesis, without surgery.  So how can this treatment protect the cartilage? Let's take a little look at the science behind what's available today in our practices and what we can apply in our offices. Hyaluronic acid.  Hyaluronic acid is a substance that already exists naturally in our joints. It is part of the novival fluid that lubricates the joints and nourishes the cartilage.  In osteoarthritis, the quantity and quality of this hyaluronic acid decrease, and the joint becomes drier, so to speak, with more friction, and the cartilage suffers more. Viscosupplementation is the replacement of this hyaluronic acid directly within the joint, guided by ultrasound, with precise accuracy.  Let's use the example of changing the oil.  Sometimes the patient jokes around, right?  It's time to lubricate, change the engine oil to reduce friction, right?  Reduce local inflammation.  Patients joke about it a lot.  But the effect goes beyond lubrication.  Studies show that hyaluronic acid has a counterprotective effect, helping to slow down cartilage degradation.  It can't regenerate completely, but it protects.  And in the scenario we're discussing, the goal is to buy time.  And that's fundamental.  Now, one development worth knowing about is injectable hydrogels, which, unlike conventional hyaluronic acid, are absorbed over a longer period, remain in the joint longer, and have a cushioning effect, filling space and absorbing impact more effectively, resulting in a greater biological effect .  That's why it's called bioactive hydrogel.  In selected cases, hydrogel can be an excellent option, especially when a longer-lasting effect is desired.  Please note that not every case is suitable for hydrogel or hyaluronic acid.  An in-person assessment is essential.  I don't want a patient with very advanced osteoarthritis to place all their hopes on a treatment without a proper evaluation and without knowing if it will work for them, and for how long it will work in their case.  That's a very important thing too.  If you have arthritis and want to know about your specific case and whether you could benefit from our services, here is the contact information for our office in São Paulo, Porto Alegre, and Telemedicine. You can schedule an evaluation to get an idea of ​​how well this treatment will work for you.  And proof of that, of its importance, is that I had this injection in my hip and in my knee.  So, two years ago, I had hyaluronic acid injections done ( there's a video on the channel), and two years later, I had bioactive hydrogel injections done in both hips and my knee.  But I only had symptoms in my left hip.  I applied it to my right hip as a preventative measure.  After all , they have the same mileage. And there are some changes that depend only on you.  Before talking about the procedure, I also have to tell you something that many patients don't want to hear, but it makes a big difference: your weight.  Every extra pound you carry multiplies the strain on your joints three to five times when you walk.  If you lose 5 kg, it's as if your joint feels like it has lost 15 to 25 kg.  And there is no injection that can compensate for a significant weight gain effect.  In other words, a person who doesn't do their part to control their weight won't reap all the benefits of a hyaluronic acid or bioactive hydrogel application. Osteoarthritis is, among other things, an inflammatory disease, and diet directly influences the level of inflammation in the body. Ultra-processed foods, excess sugar, trans fats—these things fuel inflammation.  On the other hand, fish rich in omega-3, vegetables, fruits, and olive oil are natural anti-inflammatories.   You don't need to become a nutritionist.  If you reduce ultra-processed foods and increase natural foods, you've already done a lot for your joint health.  And the third pillar is the most important of all, which is strengthening the muscles around the joint.  A strong muscle acts like a shock absorber for the joint, absorbing the impact of reactive joint forces—a concept from biomechanics.  I'm not talking about heavy weight training, exercises that can be guided by a physiotherapist, progressive strength training, water aerobics, Pilates, or even a walk.  All of this makes a measurable difference.  This has level one scientific evidence in improving osteoarthritis.  Weight, nutrition, strengthening.  These three together already slow down arthritis, but what if, in addition to slowing it down, we could reverse it?  And here I'm going to show you the Stanford study, research that could change everything we know about osteoarthritis and is causing a revolution in Silicon Valley in terms of investment in this type of research.  Remember how I said at the beginning that science is closer than ever?  So let me show you. So this 2025 study was published in Science, one of the world's most prestigious scientific journals, by researchers from Stanford University.  This researcher discovered that there is an enzyme called 15 PGDH, also known as gerozyme.  And this enzyme, it breaks down a natural substance that stimulates cartilage growth.  It's as if the body has a mechanism to regenerate cartilage, but this other enzyme, called gerozyme, acts as a brake, preventing regeneration.  What did they do?  They used a molecule that inhibits the enzyme that prevents regeneration.  And the results in experimental models were impressive.  The increase in cartilage thickness, reduction of joint damage, and most importantly, this worked in human tissue, in the laboratory.  Now we need to be transparent with you, because this is a pre-clinical study.  It has not yet been tested as a treatment for cartilage disease in patients.  This is a laboratory discovery, an experimental clinical study, tested on rats, tested on human cartilage, but in a laboratory.  But this molecule is already being used in humans for other conditions, for sarcopenia, for extreme muscle weakness.  This molecule is already being used in humans for another purpose. And this study presents something powerful and is a proof of concept. Regenerating cartilage is biologically possible.  That changes the conversation.  It's no longer a question of "if," it's a question of "when" it will happen.  And that's why protecting the cartilage you have today is the smartest decision you can make.  Because when that technology arrives, and it will arrive, you need to have cartilage to regenerate. It only works if there is cartilage to regenerate.  So let's recap.  If you have osteoarthritis, especially in the early stages and at a moderate level, your course of action is: first, don't wait for the pain to worsen.  The window of opportunity is now.  Second, consider treatments such as viscosupplementation, hyaluronic acid, and bioactive hydrogel to protect the cartilage you have. Third, invest in the three pillars of weight management, anti-inflammatory control, and muscle strengthening.  Fourth, keep your hope grounded.  Science is advancing rapidly, and your role is to get there with the best possible coordination.  If you want to know the stage of your arthritis and the best treatment plan for your specific case, you need to consult a doctor.  You can consult with me or another hip specialist in São Paulo or Porto Alegre.  I'll leave the contact information here for anyone who needs it.  If you identified with this video, or know someone who might identify with it, forward it to them, leave a like, subscribe to the channel, and let's remember, don't wait for the pain to decide for you.  Decide ahead and take control of your health.  See you in the next video.

You received an imaging exam, it could be an MRI or ultrasound, and the report showed joint effusion.  So you did what everyone else does, you searched on Google and the result: panic. Calm.  In the next few minutes I'm going to explain exactly what joint effusion means, when it's a real cause for concern, and what you should or shouldn't do right now.  Right now.  Stay with me until the end because this information will be very important for you.  My name is Davi Guzmão, I am an orthopedic surgeon specializing in the hip joint, and I bring science-based content here on YouTube so you can learn more about the hip bone and cartilage.  So the first point is not to confuse joint effusion with what is called a stroke, which is a cerebrovascular accident ( CVA).  A stroke, commonly referred to as a cerebral hemorrhage, is not the same thing as a joint hemorrhage.  Let's start with the basics.  Every joint in the body—knee, hip, shoulder—has a fluid inside called suvial fluid.  This fluid acts as a natural lubricant that our body produces.  Think of it like an oil to make the engine run, to protect an engine gear.  It's inside the joint to protect and nourish the cartilage, allowing the joint to move without friction, or rather, with as little friction as possible.  But when the report says joint effusion, it's simply saying that there is more fluid inside that joint than expected, an accumulation of fluid.  And I agree with you that the term "effusion" doesn't explain why the fluid isn't spilled inside the joint or outside the joint, although that can happen.  Basically, it means there is more fluid than expected inside that joint.  And here's the first point everyone needs to understand: joint effusion is not a diagnosis, it's a finding.  It's the same as saying that a person has a fever.  Fever is not the disease itself .  Fever is a sign that something is happening.  And joint effusion is the same way.  It is a consequence of something, not the cause itself.  So the correct question is not whether the stroke is serious.  The correct question is why is this fluid accumulating inside the joint?  It could be the hip, knee, or shoulder, right?  Any synovial joint can develop joint effusion.  Let's look at the most common causes of this happening.  Now that you understand the concept, let's think about the cause.  This is where many people are surprised, because the causes range from very simple things to situations that really need attention and could be a medical emergency.  What are the most frequent causes that are generally benign?  Osteoarthritis or arthrosis. This is by far the most common cause I see in my practice.  The joint has a degree of wear and tear, it produces a low-grade inflammatory reaction, and the body responds with increased production of synovial fluid.  It's as if the joint is trying to protect itself.  She's trying to reduce the inflammatory reaction within the joint by increasing the production of novival fluid. And it's usually a slightly less viscous fluid than usual.  It produces a thinner fluid, especially in the hip and knee; this is an extremely common finding in people over 50 who are experiencing pain in these joints. So, by far, some degree of osteoarthritis is the leading cause of joint effusion. Now there are other reasons.  Trauma or overload, a sprain, a blow, a more intense workout than usual.  The joint may become inflamed and produce more fluid, which usually improves with simple rest and ice around the joint.  The other pathology is transient synovitis.  It is much more common in children.  So, the child may sometimes wake up limping, the parents get scared and have an ultrasound done, and joint effusion is seen .  And in the vast majority of cases it is a temporary and self-limiting inflammation that resolves in days, but it requires attention because a transient synovitis may, in fact, be disguised as another pathology that we will see later.  And here, we get into issues that require further investigation.  Rheumatoid arthritis and other inflammatory diseases, especially autoimmune diseases. Stroke can be recurrent and accompanied by pain, morning stiffness, or other systemic signs.  And when we suspect that, we have to do a rheumatological evaluation.  And sometimes it may even be necessary to perform a puncture of that joint, collect the fluid, and send it for analysis to aid in the diagnosis.  Gout and pseudogout are acute effusions that often cause pain and swelling, and require specific treatment. They are frequently associated with deposits or crystals within a joint. For example, uric acid crystals are a relatively common cause of gout .  Now, the next topic is extremely important.  If you're enjoying the video so far, I invite you to subscribe to the channel so you don't miss the best information about the hip bones and cartilage.  The next piece of information relates to that pain in children, because a joint effusion can mean a joint infection, or septic arthritis.  The term septic arthritis means that there is bacteria inside the joint.  Now that 's a real emergency.  It is usually accompanied by fever, intense joint pain, it is very hot and red, and the patient is unable to move. If you're experiencing these symptoms along with joint effusion, you need to seek immediate medical attention.  So, fever, redness, severe pain along with joint effusion that appeared in some examination, require immediate medical attention.  It's an emergency, you need to go to a hospital, an emergency room.  And normally, in the hospital, more complementary tests will be done, such as a blood test, for example, to check if there is a chance of having any bacteria in that joint, and sometimes even a joint aspiration or directly a more advanced treatment such as surgical treatment.  So, realize that in the vast majority of cases, the joint effusion mentioned in the report doesn't necessarily mean something serious; it will most often be related to osteoarthritis or some degree of osteoarthritis.  So how is the doctor going to investigate this? When you arrive at the office with this report, what do I do?  Take a medical history of the patient, including when the pain started, if there was any trauma, whether the pain is constant or only appears during certain movements, and if there are other symptoms such as fever, weight loss, or morning stiffness.  Therefore, a well-conducted medical history, when discussed with the patient , will guide 80% of the medical reasoning.  Then there will be a physical exam, assessing range of motion, whether there are visible signs of inflammation , whether there is any muscle atrophy, or if, for example, in the knee, we can sometimes feel the amount of fluid in the office.  Of course, in my office I have an ultrasound machine available that allows me to visualize that joint again during the physical examination.  But lastly, we're going to do some additional tests that may be necessary. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is an excellent starting point, especially if the patient has not yet had one.  In some cases, it may be necessary to perform blood tests, inflammatory markers, uric acid tests, rheumatoid factor tests, or even, as I mentioned before, perform a joint aspiration, inserting a needle into the joint guided by ultrasound, using local anesthesia, removing a sample of the fluid and sending it for laboratory analysis; this may be part of the diagnosis.  Joint aspiration is a nearly definitive test when there is doubt about the nature of the joint effusion.  In most cases, it won't be necessary based on other aspects of the history or imaging studies; joint aspiration is usually not required, but sometimes it can be a differential diagnosis for inflammatory, infectious, or hemorrhagic joint effusion, with bleeding inside the joint.  For example, when a person experiences a very violent twist of the knee, they may tear the cruciate ligament, which can cause a more intense inflammatory reaction within the joint.  So the treatment will depend a lot on the cause of the joint effusion.  If joint effusion is related to osteoarthritis, the focus is on controlling the underlying disease.  This may involve physiotherapy, weight management, muscle strengthening, occasional use of an anti-inflammatory drug, or even the use of hyaluronic acid along with a small amount of anti-inflammatory medication, or bioactive hydrogel along with an anti-inflammatory drug when cases are more chronic.  In many cases, joint effusion will gradually disappear, especially in milder cases of osteoarthritis , naturally decreasing with these treatments.  Now, if the effusion is post-traumatic, especially in the knee, then relative rest, ice, elevation of the limb, and follow-up to ensure there is no associated structural injury, such as a meniscus tear or cruciate ligament injury, are recommended. In the case of the knee and ankle, ligament injuries are also common causes of joint effusion.  Now, if it's inflammatory, like arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, or gout, the treatment has to be specific to the disease, and the patient is usually referred to a rheumatologist.  Now, to repeat this again, because it's important, if it 's infectious, you need to be hospitalized with antibiotics, intravenous antibiotics, and in most cases, surgically lavage the joint , because this is an emergency. If bacteria remain inside the joint, they will destroy the cartilage.  That's why it's an emergency treatment.  The very important point is not to start taking anti-inflammatory drugs on your own, thinking that this alone will solve the problem.  Anti-inflammatory drugs can actually mask the symptoms and delay diagnosis, that's correct.  So, treating joint effusion without knowing the cause is like turning off a fire alarm without being sure if there's a fire somewhere. So it's a potentially dangerous thing.  So, to summarize and make life easier, I'm going to give you the warning signs, situations that, when they develop in the joints, require urgent medical evaluation .  To reiterate, symptoms include: associated fever, intense joint pain, a very swollen, hot, and red joint, inability to move or bear weight on the joint, effusion that appears suddenly without associated trauma, or effusion that recurs suddenly without explanation. Unexplained weight loss along with joint pain.  If none of these signs are present, you can relax and breathe, because most of the time joint effusion will be a finding that is part of some other broader clinical context, which requires proper guidance, a solution, or rather, an investigation .  If you have this report in hand and are still unsure, the next step is to consult an orthopedist, preferably a specialist in the joint where the problem is occurring.
